w. w. Hindman, the attorney, a
nd a member of the board of di-
rectors Of the Arcadia Orchards
Co., defends the action of the COM
pany in sending F. E. Goodall,
president of the chamber of COM
merce to New York city to adyer.
tise their lands.


Mr.. Hindman says that the en-
ployment of Mr.. Goodall was made
on the same basis as the employ
ment Of any other citizen. Goodall
is in the real estate business, and
the line of work he will carry on
in the east is in keeping with his
present occupation.


Mr.. Hindman says that the New
York exhibit will work to the great
good of the entire apple growing
district, as 1t will place the apple
display before the eyes of the pub
lie on the most important thor
oughfare in America. 90


The Arcadia Orchards Co., Mr..
Hindman says, has purchased the
holdings of the Arcadia Irrigation
association, organized in 1905 by
Floyd l.. Daggett, former mayor.
The realty holdings of the original
company have been doubled until
now the company owns 1G60O
acres, $500 acres of which are
under water. A gravity water sys-
TEN is being installed with a ditch
large enough to care for the entire
district when it is under cultiva-
ton. The company has already
expended 100000 on the project,
and proposes to invest 100000
more in the next two years.
